required.title: [Sing Together] A collection of folk songs and rounds

competence_markers.description: The child can sing several simple melodies on pitch from memory
The child can identify whether two musical excerpts are the same or different and describe what changed
The child can identify instruments by family (strings, woodwinds, brass, percussion, keyboard) in recorded music
The child can describe how a piece of music makes them feel, using specific vocabulary (not just "good" or "bad")
The child can distinguish higher from lower pitches, including pairs that are relatively close together
The child demonstrates sustained, attentive listening for age-appropriate durations (three to five minutes without distraction)
The child has listened to music from at least five different cultural traditions and can describe what they noticed about each
The child narrates what they heard in a piece of music with specific detail, not just general impressions

narration_prompts.prompt: Sing a melody you have learned. What happens to the melody — does it go up, go down, or stay the same? Where does it go the highest?
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **I am going to play you two pieces of music. Tell me: are they the same or different? If different, what changed?**

narration_prompts.prompt: What instruments did you hear in the piece we just listened to? How did you know which instruments they were?
narration_prompts.detail: 4. **Tell me about a piece of music we listened to from another part of the world. What did it sound like? How did it make you feel? Was it different from other music you know?**

narration_prompts.prompt: What happened in the piece of music you just listened to? Did it get louder or softer? Faster or slower? Did a melody come back? Tell me the story of what you heard.

cross_connections.description: Rhythm is the time dimension; pitch is the sound dimension. The two sub-domains together form the complete foundation of musical understanding. Work in both concurrently.
The singing in this packet overlaps directly with vocal work in 3.4.3-A. The child who sings melodies accurately is developing both listening and performance skills simultaneously.

The ear training here — discriminating sounds, recognizing patterns, building vocabulary — mirrors the perceptual skills developed in language acquisition. The child learning to hear musical intervals is exercising the same auditory discrimination that supports language learning.

Music from different traditions carries the values, beliefs, and emotional lives of the cultures that created it. The child listening to a hymn, a raga, and a blues recording is encountering three different worldviews expressed through sound.
Sustained listening is a focused attention exercise. The discipline of sitting still and attending to music builds the same attentional capacity that supports deep work in every domain.

content.why_this_matters:
Most people hear music. You are going to learn to listen to it.

Hearing is what happens when sound enters your ear. You cannot stop it. Listening is what happens when you pay attention — when you notice what is high and what is low, what is loud and what is soft, which instrument is playing, and how the music makes you feel. Listening is a skill, and like every skill, it gets better with practice.

In this packet, you will train your ears. You will learn to tell the difference between high sounds and low sounds, to recognize the voices of different instruments, to sing simple melodies accurately, and to listen to music from many different parts of the world. You will also begin to understand what a melody is — not just a nice tune, but a series of pitches arranged in a specific order with a specific rhythm, designed to be memorable and meaningful.

This is the beginning of a long journey. By the time you are done with all of 3.4.2, you will be able to hear inside music — to understand how it is built, why it works, and what makes the difference between music that is well-crafted and music that is merely loud. But it all starts here, with learning to listen.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Pitch discrimination** — the ability to hear high and low, and to perceive that pitch is a continuum, not just two categories
2. **Melodic memory** — the ability to sing several simple melodies accurately and to notice when a melody has been changed
3. **Analytical listening** — the habit of attending to specific elements of music (louder/softer, faster/slower, same/different) rather than just the overall impression
4. **Instrument recognition** — identifying the basic instrument families by sound
5. **Breadth of exposure** — hearing music from multiple traditions so the ear's vocabulary is broad before formal study begins
6. **Emotional vocabulary for music** — the ability to describe how music makes you feel, which is the starting point for understanding why it makes you feel that way
content.core_content:
### Part 1: High and Low — The World of Pitch

Everything that makes a musical sound vibrates. A string vibrates. Air inside a tube vibrates. A drum head vibrates. How fast it vibrates determines the **pitch** — whether the sound is high or low.

On a piano, the keys on the right play high notes and the keys on the left play low notes. If you start at the left and play every key moving right, you hear the pitch rise smoothly from very low to very high. Pitch is not just "high" and "low" — it is a continuum, like a ramp, with an infinite number of points between the bottom and the top.

Your voice has pitch too. Hum a low note — as low as you can go. Now slide your voice up, higher and higher, until you reach the highest note you can make. Everything between the bottom and the top is part of your range.

When two notes sound at the same time, they create a relationship. Some pairs of notes sound comfortable together — they "agree." Others sound tense or clashing — they "argue." Both kinds are useful in music. The comfortable ones create rest. The tense ones create energy. Music moves between tension and rest, and the movement from one to the other is one of the most powerful things about music.

### Part 2: Melody — A Journey in Pitch

A **melody** is a series of pitches, one after another, with a rhythm. It is the part of a song you sing. It is the part you remember after the song is over. A good melody is like a sentence — it has a beginning, a middle, and an end, and it makes sense as a whole.

You already know many melodies. "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." "Mary Had a Little Lamb." "Happy Birthday." These are melodies that have been passed down for generations because they are memorable and satisfying.

Try this: sing "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." Now change one note — sing one note higher or lower than it should be. Can you hear that it is wrong? Your ear already knows the melody well enough to detect a change. That is melodic memory, and you are going to develop it much further.

A melody goes up and down — sometimes by small steps (moving to the next note like walking up stairs) and sometimes by big leaps (jumping from a low note to a high note). A melody that only goes by steps sounds smooth and calm. A melody with big leaps sounds dramatic and exciting. Composers choose when to step and when to leap, and those choices are part of what gives a melody its character.

### Part 3: Same and Different — Learning to Compare

One of the most important listening skills is the ability to tell when two things are the **same** and when they are **different**. This sounds simple, but it requires real attention.

Listen to two excerpts of music. Are they the same piece? If they are different, what changed? Is the second one faster? Louder? Higher? Played on a different instrument? In a different mood?

This exercise trains your ear to attend to specific elements. Instead of just hearing music as one big sound, you begin to take it apart — noticing the speed, the volume, the pitch, the instruments, and the mood as separate things that can each change independently. This is the beginning of **analytical listening**: hearing what is happening inside the music, not just how it makes you feel.

### Part 4: Instrument Families — Who Is Playing?

Every instrument has its own voice — its own **timbre**. A violin sounds different from a trumpet, which sounds different from a flute, which sounds different from a drum. Even when they play the same note at the same volume, they sound different. Timbre is what tells your ear which instrument is which.

The main instrument families:

- **Strings** — violin, viola, cello, bass, guitar, harp. Sound is made by vibrating strings, either bowed or plucked. String instruments can sustain notes, play very expressively, and produce a wide range of volumes.
- **Woodwinds** — flute, clarinet, oboe, bassoon. Sound is made by blowing air through or across a tube. Each woodwind has a distinctive personality: the flute is bright and clear, the clarinet is warm and versatile, the oboe is nasal and penetrating, the bassoon is deep and sometimes comical.
- **Brass** — trumpet, trombone, French horn, tuba. Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a metal mouthpiece. Brass instruments can be powerful and brilliant or soft and warm, depending on how they are played.
- **Percussion** — drums, xylophone, cymbals, triangle, timpani. Sound is made by striking, shaking, or scraping. Percussion instruments provide rhythm, color, and punctuation.
- **Keyboard** — piano, organ, harpsichord. The piano is in a category of its own: it can play melody and accompaniment at the same time, play very loud and very soft, and sustain notes or make them short. It is the most versatile single instrument.
- **Voice** — the instrument every person carries. Voices are classified by range: soprano (high female), alto (low female), tenor (high male), bass (low male).

Listen to *Peter and the Wolf*. Prokofiev assigns each character a specific instrument: the bird is the flute, the duck is the oboe, the cat is the clarinet, the grandfather is the bassoon, the wolf is the French horns, Peter is the string section. After listening, you should be able to identify these instruments when you hear them in other music.

### Part 5: Music from Everywhere

Music is not one thing. It is thousands of things — different in every culture, every tradition, every part of the world. The rhythm of a West African drum ensemble sounds nothing like a Viennese waltz. A Japanese shakuhachi flute sounds nothing like a Scottish bagpipe. Indian classical music uses notes between the notes that Western pianos cannot even play.

In this packet, you will listen to music from many traditions. You do not need to understand everything you hear. You need to hear it — to let your ears absorb the sounds, the rhythms, the timbres, and the feelings. This broad exposure builds your ear's vocabulary. A person who has heard only one kind of music can only listen to that kind of music. A person who has heard music from around the world can listen to anything.

For each piece you hear, describe what you notice: Is it fast or slow? Loud or soft? Does it use instruments you recognize? How does it make you feel? What does it remind you of? There are no wrong answers at this stage. Your honest perceptions are the starting point for everything that comes later.

### Part 6: Listening with Your Whole Attention

Here is the hardest skill in this packet: sitting still and listening to a piece of music with your whole attention, from beginning to end, without doing anything else.

No drawing while listening. No fidgeting. No talking. Just listening. This is hard. It requires the same kind of focus that reading a book requires — your mind must be on the music, following it as it unfolds, noticing what happens.

Start short. Listen to a single movement of a piece — three to five minutes. Over time, your listening stamina will grow, just like your reading stamina grows over time. The goal is not to sit still for an hour. The goal is to build the habit of sustained attention to music, so that when you encounter a great piece of music, you are able to receive what it offers.

After listening, narrate: what did you hear? What happened in the music? Did it get louder? Did it change mood? Did you hear a melody come back after going away? Your narration is your proof of listening — it shows what you noticed.
content.formative_practices:
### Activity 1: High-Low Exploration [Early Packet]
At a piano or xylophone, play notes from low to high and back. Sing along. Play two notes and have the child identify which is higher. Play pairs that are far apart first (easy), then closer together (harder). This trains pitch discrimination.

### Activity 2: Melody Singing [Ongoing]
Learn at least six simple melodies by heart: folk songs, hymns, nursery rhymes from different traditions. Sing them regularly. The mentor should sing with the child, modeling pitch accuracy without making it a test.

### Activity 3: Same or Different? [Ongoing]
Play two short musical excerpts. The child identifies: same or different? If different: what changed? Start with obvious differences (different instruments, very different tempos) and progress to subtle ones (same melody in a different key, same piece slightly faster).

### Activity 4: Instrument Identification [Mid-Packet]
Listen to *Peter and the Wolf* and identify each instrument. Then listen to other recordings and identify the instrument families: "I hear strings." "That's a brass instrument." "There's a drum." Build this skill gradually.

### Activity 5: World Music Listening Sessions [Ongoing]
Once per week, listen to music from a tradition different from the child's own. A Vivaldi concerto. A West African drum ensemble. A Hindustani raga. A blues recording. A Japanese koto piece. A hymn. A folk song from the Appalachian or Celtic tradition. For each, the child narrates what they noticed.

### Activity 6: Focused Listening [Ongoing — Building Stamina]
Listen to a short piece of music (three to five minutes) with full attention. No other activity. Afterward, the child narrates what they heard: what happened? Did the music change? What instruments did you hear? What mood did it create? Increase the duration gradually as the child's attention span develops.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Pitch Discrimination
**Developing well:** The child can identify which of two notes is higher, even when they are relatively close together. They can sing the starting pitch of a song when given it, and their singing stays on pitch.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot consistently identify higher and lower, or their singing is notably off-pitch despite regular practice.

**Diagnostic:** Some children need more time with physical pitch demonstrations. Play notes on a xylophone and let the child see the physical relationship (bigger bars = lower, smaller bars = higher) while hearing it. The visual-auditory connection sometimes unlocks pitch perception.

### 2. Melodic Memory
**Developing well:** The child can sing at least six melodies accurately from memory. When a melody is altered, they notice and can identify the change.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot remember melodies or sings them with significant pitch errors.

**Diagnostic:** Are you singing together regularly — daily or near-daily? Melodic memory develops through repetition. If the child is only hearing songs once, they will not retain them.

### 3. Analytical Listening
**Developing well:** When comparing two excerpts, the child can identify specific differences: "this one is faster," "different instrument," "louder at the end." Their narrations after listening include specific observations.

**Developing slowly:** Comparisons are vague ("they sound different"). Narrations are one-sentence summaries without detail.

**Diagnostic:** Are your listening questions specific enough? Instead of "what did you notice?" try "was this piece fast or slow?" or "did you hear strings or brass?" Specific questions develop specific listening skills.

### 4. Sustained Attention
**Developing well:** The child can listen to a three-to-five-minute piece with full attention and narrate what they heard with detail.

**Developing slowly:** The child fidgets, talks, or loses focus within the first minute.

**Diagnostic:** Is the listening session happening at a time when the child is alert? Are the pieces appropriate for the child's attention level? Start with shorter, more dramatic pieces (a single movement of a concerto, a short folk piece) and build toward longer works.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Background Music Instead of Focused Listening
Playing music while the child draws or eats is pleasant but it is not the training this packet requires. Focused listening means the music is the primary activity — the child sits and listens with full attention. Background music is fine in addition to focused listening, but it is not a substitute.

### Western-Only Listening
The temptation to play only classical and pop music is strong because it is familiar. Resist it. The child's ear needs to absorb sounds from the world, not just from one tradition. A child who has never heard a sitar, a djembe, or a shakuhachi has a limited auditory vocabulary.

### Testing Instead of Experiencing
Do not turn listening sessions into quizzes. The comparison exercises (same or different?) should feel like games, not tests. If the child experiences listening as evaluation, they will learn to dread it. Listening should be pleasurable, interesting, and participatory.

### Neglecting Singing
Some mentors focus on the listening exercises and skip the singing. Do not. Singing is the most important activity in this packet because it develops pitch accuracy, melodic memory, and musical confidence simultaneously. The child who sings regularly is training their ear far more effectively than the child who only listens.
